blocks.text: Orson Pratt’s 1876 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, revised in 1879 to match more closely the 1879 edition of the Book of Mormon, became the standard for more than forty years. During that time, some changes were made in various printings, but another major edition of the Doctrine and Covenants did not appear until 1921, once again in conjunction with a new edition of the Book of Mormon.

The new Book of Mormon edition came out in 1920 and, as with the Doctrine and Covenants, was the first major edition of that scriptural work since Orson Pratt’s 1879 version. The committee of apostles who produced the 1920 Book of Mormon included George F. Richards as chair and Anthony W. Ivins, Joseph Fielding Smith, James E. Talmage, and Melvin J. Ballard as members. In February 1921, Elder Talmage wrote to fellow apostle George Albert Smith about what the Book of Mormon committee had in mind to do next. “Preliminary steps have already been taken toward a thorough revision of the Doctrine and Covenants,” he disclosed, explaining that the editions printed in the United States and England contained “many errors by way of omission.” Elder Talmage also felt he and his committee members could improve the section headings, expand the notes, and make the text appear more pleasing by structuring it in double columns as they had done for the Book of Mormon.

These ideas needed approval from the First Presidency, which underwent change when first counselor Anthon H. Lund died on March 2, 1921. Eight days later, Charles W. Penrose, who had been serving as second counselor to President Heber J. Grant, became first counselor, and Book of Mormon committee member Anthony W. Ivins became second counselor. The next day, March 11, committee chair George F. Richards wrote in his journal that the First Presidency had heard the committee’s recommendations and “what has been known as the Book of Mormon Committee” could now be styled “the Doctrine & Covenants Committee.”

For the 1876 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, Orson Pratt had added twenty-six new sections. In 1879, he had also proposed incorporating parts of The Pearl of Great Price (which had not yet been canonized, or accepted as a book of scripture) and deleting the Lectures on Faith (which had been bound in with the Doctrine and Covenants since 1835). His 1879 proposal had not been accepted. Instead, in October 1880, The Pearl of Great Price had become the Church’s fourth standard work when a general conference voted to accept it as a book of scripture.

The 1921 Doctrine and Covenants committee was equally bold in its suggestions. On July 29, 1921, Elder Richards recorded in his journal that the committee met and “read the revelations which do not appear in the present edition of the Doctrine & Covenants, about twenty in number[,] with the view of recommending to the First Presidency certain of them to be included in the edition we are just now preparing.” The First Presidency did not approve any additional revelations for inclusion in the main body of the volume, leaving the total number of sections at 136. Wilford Woodruff’s 1890 Manifesto regarding plural marriage, however, which had been appended to Latter-day Saint editions of the Doctrine and Covenants since 1908, was also included in the 1921 edition under the heading “Official Declaration.”

The biggest change in the content of the 1921 edition was actually a deletion. As the preface to the edition explained, “Certain lessons, entitled ‘Lectures on Faith,’ which were bound in with the Doctrine and Covenants in some of its former issues, are not included in this edition. Those lessons were prepared for use in the School of the Elders, conducted in Kirtland, Ohio, during the winter of 1834–1835; but they were never presented to nor accepted by the Church as being otherwise than theological lectures or lessons.”

Removing the Lectures on Faith from the Doctrine and Covenants posthumously fulfilled a desire of Orson Pratt, who had proposed the change in 1879. When making this recommendation, Elder Pratt had written, “If it were deemed necessary at any future time, to republish the ‘Lectures on Faith,’ it could be done in pamphlet form.” The lectures have been published separately from the Doctrine and Covenants many times over the ensuing decades.

Another significant change to the 1921 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants was the arrangement of the text in a double-column format to match the 1920 edition of the Book of Mormon, which had been arranged in that form to make it more like commonly available Bibles. The committee also expanded the headnotes to the sections of the Doctrine and Covenants and revised the footnotes as well.

On December 17, 1921—just eight days before Christmas—the First Presidency announced the new edition of the Doctrine and Covenants, as well as a new version of the Pearl of Great Price, “all conforming in general to the style and arrangement of the latest text of the Book of Mormon.”

After describing the features of the new edition of the Doctrine and Covenants—including the “Explanatory Introduction,” a “Chronological Order of Contents,” and the much-expanded “Index and Concordance”—the First Presidency explained the three new styles in which the Doctrine and Covenants was now available.

The first was a “Library edition,” which corresponded “in typography, material, and workmanship to the Library edition of the Book of Mormon.” The second was a double-combination edition of the Doctrine and Covenants and the Pearl of Great Price “on thin paper,” with “flexible leather binding, forming a companion volume to the India paper issue of the Book of Mormon.” The third was a triple combination—Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price—also “on India paper” with a “flexible leather binding.”

The members of the First Presidency—Heber J. Grant, Charles W. Penrose, and Anthony W. Ivins—closed their announcement letter with an exhortation to all: “We recommend these new issues of the three distinctive standard works of the Church for thoughtful and prayerful study.”

With this announcement, the members of the 1921 Doctrine and Covenants Committee could fully enjoy their Christmas, knowing they had completed their assignment well. Their work on the book of scripture would stand for more than half a century.
